title: ComfyUI Tutorial Series Ep 31: ComfyUI Tips & Tricks You Need to Know
description:
In this episode, we cover 27 practical tips and tricks to help you get the most out of ComfyUI. Learn how to manually update the software, organize your workflows, customize settings, manage nodes, troubleshoot issues, and even optimize performance on lower-end hardware. Whether you're a beginner or experienced user, these tips will make your ComfyUI experience smoother and more efficient.

Get the workflows and instructions from discord
https://discord.gg/gggpkVgBf3

Check Other Episodes
https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL-pohOSaL8P9kLZP8tQ1K1QWdZEgwiBM0

Unlock exclusive perks by joining our channel:
https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCmMbwA-s3GZDKVzGZ-kPwaQ/join

#comfyui #aitips #tipsandtricks
transcript: Welcome to episode 31 of our Comfy UI tutorial series.  Today, I will show you some tips and tricks inside Comfy UI  that I consider useful to know.  If you open Comfy UI, you can go to the manager.  And you should already know that clicking on Update Comfy UI  updates your version of Comfy UI.  Clicking on Update All updates Comfy UI plus the nodes.  But did you know that sometimes the manager fails to update  to the latest version if you scroll down on the right?  You can see the Comfy UI version and the manager version.  The best way to check is by looking at the date to see  if it's recent from the last few days.  For example, this version is from January 13th.  To update Comfy UI manually, go to your Comfy UI folder  where you usually have the Bat file that starts Comfy UI.  There you'll find a folder named Update.  Inside, there are three files.  The first file updates Comfy UI.  The second is useful if Comfy UI doesn't start.  For example, if you've installed new nodes or dependencies,  as this file can sometimes fix that issue.  The third file allows you to revert to a stable version of Comfy UI  if the updated version has bugs.  Open the Update Comfy UI Bat file  and you'll see something happen on the screen.  Press any key to exit.  Then you can go back and start Comfy UI normally  and it will install the updates.  If I go back to the manager now,  I can check and see that there is a more recent version  from yesterday in my case.  Most of the time, the manager works fine,  but just in case your Comfy UI doesn't update,  it's useful to know you can do it manually.  On the bottom left, you have the settings.  Here, you'll find an option called New Menu.  You can move the new menu to the top or bottom.  If you disable it, you'll get the old menu.  If you started with episode one,  you might remember that a few episodes ago  we had a different menu, the old one.  So if the new menu is disabled,  you'll see that old version  which had the settings button here.  For a few episodes,  I've used the new menu since it's more useful to me  and I have it at the top.  Speaking of the top menu,  you can see that I have some extra information  showing how much memory is used,  CPU usage, and the temperature.  This gives you an idea of what happens  when you use complex workflows.  When I run flux models,  it can go up to 100% with video RAM,  but I usually just keep an eye on the temperature  to avoid overheating.  I have a good cooling system,  so I've never had a problem with temperature.  To activate this extra information on the bar,  go to the manager,  then the custom nodes manager  and search for crystals.  Click the install button.  Mine is already installed,  but you should see the install option.  After that, restart comfy UI,  and it should appear on the bar.  If, for some reason,  it still doesn't appear,  some users have had glitches.  In that case, you can go to settings,  change the new menu position to the bottom,  then back to the top,  and that should force it to appear.  You can see here on the top left,  I have this bar where I can open multiple workflows at once,  just like opening different images in Photoshop.  To activate that,  go to settings,  then search for workflow.  You'll find an option called open new workflow position,  which is usually set to sidebar,  so the workflows open on the left.  If you choose this,  you'll see it disappear from the top,  but I prefer to have it on the top bar,  so just choose what works best for you.  To add a new node,  you can double-click on the canvas.  On the left, you'll see a preview of the node,  and on the right,  you'll see which node packet belongs to.  If it says comfy core,  it means it comes with comfy UI.  If it has another name,  that's the name of a custom node pack  installed in your comfy UI.  Once I add a node here,  or if you open a workflow from someone else,  and aren't sure which node packet came from,  here's what I like to do.  Go to settings,  search for badge,  and enable the option that shows the node source.  If it's set to show all,  it will display information on top of each node,  both for comfy UI and custom nodes.  However, I prefer to only see custom nodes,  so I select hide built-in.  When I close the settings,  you'll see that the node now has extra text showing  which custom node packet belongs to.  For example,  if I add another node,  it will display the custom node name.  But if I add a node that comes with comfy UI,  it doesn't show anything,  so I know it's part of comfy UI.  If I open a workflow from episode three,  you can see that all the nodes are comfy UI nodes  because there's no extra information  on the top right of the nodes.  If I move a node,  you can see that the nodes are connected using these links,  or cables, or spaghetti.  You can hide these links if you want  by using the eye button in this menu,  but you can also go to settings  and select the light graph option,  where you'll find the link render mode.  By default, it's set to spline.  If I change it to straight and check the workflow,  now you'll see that all the lines are straight.  They adjust when you move the nodes,  but still remains straight.  If I change it to linear,  the lines become straight,  but will go at an angle from one node to another.  The last option, hidden,  works just like the option I showed earlier,  which hides the links.  I'll keep it set to the default, which is spline.  If you go to workflows,  you have the option to save or export the workflow.  If you choose export,  you can give it a name,  save it somewhere on your hard drive,  and send it to others if you want.  Alternatively, you can use save or save as to give it a name,  and it will be saved in a folder inside comfy UI.  If you go to the workflow sidebar,  you can see your save workflows there.  For example, I have some workflows from episode 10.  I can copy them,  go to the comfy UI folder,  then the user's folder,  default, and finally workflows.  There, you'll see the workflow I just saved.  You can also create a folder  and place the workflows you want inside it.  After that, restart comfy UI.  Once restarted, if I go to workflows,  you'll find the folder I added with episode 10,  and you can click on a workflow to open it.  Just make sure to add only the workflow files,  not the images.  As you can see, I added an image here,  and it can get confusing.  If I click on another workflow,  it opens in the top bar as well.  Also, on the right side of each workflow,  there's a bookmark icon.  If you click on it, it will add the workflow to the top  as a bookmark,  making it easy to access your favorites.  If you click the bookmark icon again,  it will remove it from the bookmarks list.  So, organize your workflows and folders,  or use bookmarks for easy access.  If I hold the control key,  I can click and drag with the mouse to select multiple nodes.  Then, by pressing the shift key,  I can move all those nodes around.  But sometimes, it's easier to put them into a group.  If I right-click on the canvas,  I can add a group for the selected nodes.  Now, I can move the entire group  with all the nodes at once.  Plus, you'll have access to more options for groups.  You can easily rename a group  by double-clicking on the title and giving it a name.  You can do the same for the node as well,  making it easier to remember what each node does.  I prefer to leave the original name  and maybe add some extra information after it.  You can also create a group first  and then add your nodes inside it.  Let's say I want to rename the first group  as text-to-image and the second one as upscale.  Then, I can add a node that's part of the RG3 custom node pack  called FastGroupMutter.  If you don't have it,  make sure you install RG3 first  from the manager and custom nodes.  This node gives us a switch  that lets us mute or deactivate the nodes inside the group,  allowing us to build more complex workflows  and enable only the parts we want.  Let me just add a node inside this group  to see how it's disabled.  The switch has two options.  Yes and no.  When it's set to yes, it's enabled  and when it's set to no, it's disabled.  Right now, it shows both groups.  But for example, I can right-click on the second group,  go to edit group and change the color to red.  Now, if I go to the FastGroupMutter  and in the properties filter to show only one color  that matches this field,  I can set it to red.  In this switch, I'll now only see the red group.  So, I can make the switch specific to this group.  I can even change the color of the node to red.  So, it matches.  If I want, I can make a copy of the node  change the color so it's not confusing  and then go to the properties.  For the match color, I can use pale blue,  which is the color of default groups.  Now, this switch will only show that group.  If I remove the match color,  it will show both groups again.  Lastly, if you modify a workflow and then close it,  it will ask if you want to save the workflow.  I won't save it in this case.  Let's say I run this a workflow  and at the end, we have the save image node  which saves the image.  By default, it has the prefix comfy UI.  The images are saved in the output folder  and you can see here that they have the comfy UI prefix.  However, you can change that to something else.  For example, I can change it to robot.  Now, when I run the workflow,  the prefix for the image will be robot,  making it easier to search for certain images.  If I hover over it, it suggests other options I can use.  For instance, I can add the date in the format year, month, and day.  Now, when I run the workflow,  the image will have the date included in its name,  so I can sort them by date.  Alternatively, I can create a folder with that date  and inside that folder,  the images will be saved with the prefix IMG.  Now, when I run the workflow,  I can find the folder with the current date  and inside the images will have the prefix I set in the save node.  The first part can also be the folder name.  For example, if I want a folder named Portrait,  I add a forward slash and the text I want  in the image prefix like robot.  After I run it, I get the Portrait folder  and inside the image will have the robot prefix.  When you run a workflow,  you don't always know exactly what the result will be  in terms of composition, colors, etc.  So you have to wait until it's done to see what you get.  If you're running a large model like Flux,  it can take a while.  It would be nice to have a preview, right?  If you go to the manager,  by default, the preview is set to none  because it's faster not to show a preview.  But you can try different preview methods  to see which one works better for your needs.  For example, let's choose auto.  Now, when I run the workflow,  take a look at what happens in the K-sampler.  It shows a preview of how the image is being generated  before you get the final result.  This way, you can have an idea of whether it will work or not.  You can run it and even cancel it if you don't like it.  For a large model like SDXL,  it's probably not worth it since it's already fast.  But for other models, the preview can be very useful.  In the top left, you have a queue button  with a clock that goes backward.  It shows everything you've generated so far,  including the things you've canceled.  You can see all these robots listed there.  If I click on the eye icon,  I can see a bigger preview of the image,  but you can also load the workflow from that image.  I can right-click on it and select load workflow.  This will give me the exact workflow  that was used with the same prompt  and seed.  If I run it, it will generate the exact same robot.  Let me go to the manager and disable the preview.  But now, the second time I run it,  I'll get a different robot  because the seed was set to randomize.  So when you load the workflow for the first time,  you can set it to a fixed seed if you want.  Then you can play with the settings  to get subtle variations of the original robot.  When you have a weak video card with low video RAM,  sometimes it can crash at the VAE Decode node.  To help avoid that crash,  you can replace it with the VAE Decode Tiled version instead.  This version offers more options,  including the ability to reduce the tile size,  making it more manageable even on systems  with lower video RAM.  You can just redo the connections  and it should work as a replacement  for the original node by default.  It's set to 512 pixels for the tile size,  but you can lower it further.  If your system still crashes,  keep in mind that when it works without crashing,  it will take more time to run  because it processes multiple smaller tiles  instead of one larger tile.  When you use a save image node,  all the images are saved in the output folder.  However, you can also use a preview node  to decide which images to save.  You can right-click and save the image from the preview.  Now, sometimes you generate another image and think,  you know what, the image I generated before was better,  but is the previous image lost?  Actually no.  If you go to your comfy Ui folder,  you'll find a folder called temp.  This is a temporary folder where all the preview images  are saved.  For example, if I close comfy Ui,  I thought the images would be deleted  since it's only temporary folder,  but they're still there.  However, when I start comfy Ui, again,  the images are deleted.  So the temp folder keeps the temporary images,  like those from previews until you open a new comfy Ui session.  In the output folder, you'll find all the images saved  using the save image node,  as well as audio or video files.  Everything goes to the output folder.  You also have an input folder.  Here, for example, you can find the images you've pasted  into the load image node.  If I add a load image node now,  it can load an image like this one with the birds.  But if I delete those and the pasted folder,  then when I add the load image node,  there won't be anything there.  You can use this arrow to navigate through all the images  in that folder.  In my case, I only have the example image.  Sometimes you need to add notes in the workflow.  For example, you can add a node node  and include extra information for yourself  or others who may use your workflows,  but sometimes you just need short labels  instead of long notes.  If you have the RG3 node installed,  you can use the label node.  You can move it around freely,  and if you right-click, go to properties  and change the font size.  However, I prefer to open the properties panel  where I can adjust the font size and font color,  either by using a color code or the color name.  When you have text on multiple lines,  you can also align it.  The background of the label can be transparent,  or you can choose a color for the background.  This makes it easier to customize labels for clarity  and organization in your workflow.  Sometimes you don't need all the information from nodes.  You set them once and forget about them.  Do you see this gray dot on each node?  If you click on it, you can collapse that node,  making the workflow look cleaner.  If you click it again, it will expand.  You can also do this by right-clicking and choosing collapse,  and you can expand it again using the right-click menu  and selecting expand.  This feature is useful for organizing your workflow  and making it more compact,  especially when dealing with complex setups.  You know that you can select a node  and use Ctrl plus C to copy that node,  then Ctrl plus V to paste it.  You can also hold Ctrl and select multiple nodes  then copy and paste all of them.  After that, if you hold Shift,  you can move all those nodes at once.  However, as you can see,  some of the links are lost in the process.  You may not need those links,  especially if you want to connect them to other workflows,  but if you want to keep the links from the original node  after you copied the nodes,  instead of using Ctrl plus V,  use Ctrl plus Shift plus V.  This will paste the nodes and keep the links  from the first node.  Now, you can generate and get two images.  You can change the key-sampler settings  to get two different variations.  This is a helpful method for keeping node connections intact  while making copies of nodes  for different parts of your workflow.  You should know by now that each PNG image you generate  also contains the workflow used to generate it.  So I can drag that image into ComfyUI  and it will load the workflow for me  with all the nodes, prompts, and settings that were used.  This allows you to regenerate the exact same image  or adjust the settings if you want.  Now, sometimes this is true for things  where other people upload online,  but some websites strip the metadata from the image.  However, for example, Discord doesn't strip it.  So if I go to one of my channels on Discord  and someone uploaded a PNG image,  it should have the workflow incorporated  as long as it wasn't edited in Photoshop  or saved in a different format.  You can click on the image, then select Open in Browser.  And from there, you can drag it into the tab  where your ComfyUI is running.  When you release it, it will open a new workflow tab  with all the nodes and settings used  to generate that image.  This can be very useful, but if you don't want that behavior,  you can either use a node that doesn't save the workflow  or you can just save the image as a JPG format  using Photoshop or another tool.  Let me try with a different image like this one.  This one also contains the workflow,  but it's missing some nodes that I don't have.  You could go to the manager and click  install the missing nodes to install the nodes  that are missing.  Now, this doesn't guarantee the workflow will work perfectly.  First, you need to have the same models  and you might have different dependencies installed,  but at least you can get an idea  of what nodes, prompts, and settings were used.  When you have multiple workflows open,  you can go to the one you want to keep,  then select close other tabs.  So you are left with only the preferred workflow.  For the next tip, I'll use a node that I use  in episode 15, which you can install  from the manager called iTools.  From this node, I can add something like the line loader node,  which allows you to add multiple prompts on different lines.  Until now, when you dragged a link from other nodes,  you couldn't connect it directly.  But now, a little dot appears under the clip field,  which transforms the text into an input.  So you can connect it.  Let me undo that by converting it back to a widget.  Before, we used to do this by converting the widget  to input before we could link it.  Now, you can just drag a link, and if it's possible,  it will automatically convert the widget to an input.  So you can link it.  For example, C0 is the first prompt, the cat.  You can change it to increment,  and it will take each prompt in order  every time you run the workflow.  So if I run the workflow now, it will take the cat.  If I run it again, it will move to the second prompt,  the dog, and so on.  Alternatively, you can make it go automatically,  change it to queue instant, and run it.  It will keep generating a forever cycling  through all the prompts in a loop.  It will not stop until you go back and select queue,  and it finishes the last seed that was still working on.  Sometimes, when you've been working for a while,  or if you have a lower end video card,  or multiple workflows open, the models  can get stuck in memory.  A restart of comfy UI can fix that,  but you can also try this button that says unload models.  For example, right now, my VRAM usage is at 37%,  but if I click unload models, it goes down to 11%,  so when you run a workflow, it will reload those models,  and the VRAM usage will go up again.  Additionally, there are nodes you can add to your workflow  that will clear VRAM after each run.  This can help prevent VRAM from building up too much  over time and keep things running smoothly.  But it will also take more time  because it needs to reload the model each time  instead of having the model in memory.  If your comfy UI is updated, you should see this button here.  When you click on it, it will open a panel  that shows the information you would normally see  in the command window, such as what models loaded,  how much time it took, errors,  and all kinds of useful information,  especially when you encounter errors.  Next to it, you have a button to hide the menu.  Speaking of errors, if you go to the manager  and custom nodes manager, you'll notice that each custom node  has a blue title.  If I click on one of those nodes,  I can go to that node's GitHub page.  There, I can find more information about the node,  what dependencies it needs,  and maybe even some fixes for your errors.  On the top left, you have a tab called issues  where you can see all the open issues.  If I search for something here,  it will show any open issues that contain that word.  Typically, you would write your error message here.  If you still can't find it in the open issues,  you can remove this filter, which searches only open issues,  and then search for your error across all posted issues.  You can click on one of those links  and read the comments to see if anyone has found a solution.  Alternatively, you can click on new issue  and post about your own issue.  Also, on my free Discord server, besides the workflows,  I have a channel called ComfyUI, frequently asked questions  where I add possible solutions to different errors people  encounter when using some of the workflows.  For example, some people recently had issues  with nodes that couldn't be installed.  In one of the posts on Discord,  I uploaded a short video showing how to use  the installer created by Ivo,  which automatically installs ComfyUI  and the nodes I use in my workflows.  Some people had problems with crystals  or the certain node because the node couldn't be installed  due to different dependency requirements.  So using this installer can help fix some of those issues.  Sometimes when you open a workflow from someone else  or from the internet, you may try to move the nodes  around and find that you can.  For example, I try to move this node,  but it doesn't work while this one works just fine.  This happens because the node is pinned.  You can right click on it and select unpin  and then you'll be able to move it around.  Some people might hide other nodes behind these pin nodes.  So beginners can't easily see which nodes are used  or for other reasons though I've never really understood  why some people do that.  You can also play around with ComfyUI's themes.  You can toggle between dark or light themes from here.  I'll leave it on dark for now.  Then you can go to settings and search for appearance.  In this section, you'll find the color palette  where you can select light or solarized if you prefer.  When I close it, you can see the colors here.  Let's go back and see what other options we have.  There are a few more dark themes including  arc, Nord, and one called GitHub.  Simply scroll down to reveal additional themes.  Also, if you go to settings,  it's not just ComfyUI that has settings here.  Some nodes like RG3 also have extra settings  that you can change.  For example, crystals has some options  that you can enable or disable from here.  You also have the key binding section  where you can see the shortcuts that are being used.  From here, you can remove existing shortcuts or add your own.  Then there's the about section  where you can see more information  about your ComfyUI version.  What front end you're using,  which Python version is installed,  and in other details that can be useful  for troubleshooting or understanding your setup.  If you have a portable ComfyUI setup like I do,  and as I showed you in episode one,  that means you have this Python embedded folder.  This means your Python is embedded here  and it installs the right version it needs.  It also installs its own environment  so that ComfyUI can work without the need  to install Python separately.  While you're in this folder,  you can go to the address bar, type C, MD,  and then press Enter.  This will open the command window  in that exact same location,  making it easier to run commands directly from there  without having to navigate to the folder manually.  The command python.xc-mpiplist is used  to display a list of all installed Python packages  in the current environment.  The python.xc runs python while the dash M flag  ensures that the PIP module is executed  within the correct Python environment.  The list argument tells PIP  to show all installed packages,  making it useful for checking dependencies  or troubleshooting installation issues.  You can also use a command with PIP show  to display information for a specific package  instead of getting the entire list.  This way you can check on the custom notes pages  to see what packages they need  and upgrading or downgrading some of these packages  might help fix the problem.  I don't know too much about Python either,  but I have chat GPT to help out when needed  and it can assist with Python related questions  or troubleshooting from time to time.  Since we already have Python installed,  you can run Python code directly as comfort UI  and the notes contain a lot of Python code.  You can also ask chat GPT how to run certain Python scripts  if you're not sure.  For example, I can open a notepad file,  paste the Python code into it,  and then when I save it, I select all files  instead of text documents.  For the file name, I add the .py extension  as that's the format Python uses.  I'll save it in the Python embedded folder,  so I don't have to look for it later.  Now, if I open the command window in this folder,  I can type python.exe followed by the file name  and it will show me the result of that code.  Like you can see here, you can edit the code  with notepad, change something and save it.  When you run the code again, you'll see the updated result here  and with that, it looks like it's time to end this video.  Thank you, Legends and VIPs for your support on this channel  and everyone who helps by subscribing  or sending a super thanks  so I can keep bringing you new episodes each week.  If you found some useful tips, please leave a like  and a comment to help boost the YouTube algorithm.  Thank you, have a great day and I'll see you on Discord.
